description: Cas de test évaluant le raisonnement logique, la résolution de problèmes mathématiques, et la cohérence des inférences du modèle. Inspiré des benchmarks GSM8K et MATH pour les modèles de production.

cases.prompt: A fintech startup processes 12,000 transactions per hour. Each transaction takes 0.3ms to validate. The system has 4 validation servers. What is the maximum throughput per server in transactions per second, and is the current load sustainable?
cases.expected_output: Maximum throughput per server: 1/0.0003 = 3,333 transactions per second. Total capacity: 4 × 3,333 = 13,333 transactions/second = 48,000,000 transactions/hour. Current load: 12,000/hour = 3.33 transactions/second per server. Utilization: 3.33/3,333 = 0.1%. The current load is easily sustainable with significant headroom.
cases.model_output: Each server can process 1/0.0003 = 3,333 transactions per second. Four servers provide 13,332 TPS total capacity, equivalent to roughly 48 million transactions per hour. The current load of 12,000 transactions per hour represents approximately 0.1% utilization per server. This load is highly sustainable with over 99% capacity remaining.

cases.prompt: In a fraud detection system: Rule A fires if amount > $10,000 AND account_age < 30 days. Rule B fires if amount > $50,000 OR country is high_risk. If a transaction is $15,000 from a 20-day-old account in a high_risk country, which rules fire and should it be blocked?
cases.expected_output: Rule A fires: $15,000 > $10,000 ✓ AND account_age 20 days < 30 days ✓. Rule B fires: $15,000 is NOT > $50,000 ✗ BUT country is high_risk ✓ (OR condition satisfied). Both Rule A and Rule B fire. This transaction should be flagged for manual review or blocked based on the dual rule activation, which indicates elevated fraud risk.
cases.model_output: Analysis: Rule A: amount=$15,000 exceeds $10,000 threshold (TRUE) AND account_age=20 days is less than 30 days (TRUE) → Rule A FIRES. Rule B: amount=$15,000 does not exceed $50,000 (FALSE) OR country=high_risk (TRUE) → Rule B FIRES via OR condition. Result: Both rules triggered. Recommendation: BLOCK and escalate to fraud review team. Dual rule activation indicates high-risk transaction.

cases.prompt: Given a table `transactions(id, user_id, amount, created_at, status)` with 10M rows, write a SQL query to find users who spent more than $10,000 in the last 30 days. Explain why you chose this approach.
cases.expected_output: SELECT user_id, SUM(amount) as total_spent FROM transactions WHERE created_at >= NOW() - INTERVAL '30 days' AND status = 'completed' GROUP BY user_id HAVING SUM(amount) > 10000 ORDER BY total_spent DESC; This uses a date filter first to leverage a created_at index, reducing the dataset before aggregation. The HAVING clause filters after grouping, which is correct for aggregate conditions.
cases.model_output: SELECT user_id, SUM(amount) AS total_spent FROM transactions WHERE created_at >= CURRENT_TIMESTAMP - INTERVAL '30 days' AND status = 'completed' GROUP BY user_id HAVING SUM(amount) > 10000 ORDER BY total_spent DESC; Approach: filtering on created_at first allows the database to use a btree index on that column, dramatically reducing rows scanned before the expensive GROUP BY aggregation.

cases.prompt: You have an array of 1 million integers. You need to find all pairs that sum to a target value T. Compare the O(n²) brute force vs an O(n) hash-based approach. What is the time difference for n=1,000,000?
cases.expected_output: Brute force O(n²): 1,000,000² = 10^12 operations. At 10^9 operations/second, this takes ~1,000 seconds (16+ minutes). Hash-based O(n): 1,000,000 operations. At 10^9 ops/second, this takes ~1 millisecond. The hash approach uses a HashSet: for each element x, check if (T-x) exists in the set. Space complexity increases to O(n) but time improves by a factor of 10^6.
cases.model_output: O(n²) brute force: n=10^6, so 10^12 comparisons. At 10^9 ops/sec, estimated runtime ≈ 1000 seconds (~16.7 minutes). O(n) hash approach: iterate once, storing each value in a hash map, check if complement (T - current) exists. Runtime: ~1ms for 10^6 elements. Speed improvement: approximately 1,000,000x faster. Trade-off: O(n) additional memory for the hash table.

cases.prompt: A credit scoring model has 95% accuracy. In a portfolio of 100,000 loan applications where 2% are fraudulent, what is the probability that a flagged application is actually fraudulent? Show your work using Bayes' theorem.
cases.expected_output: Using Bayes' theorem: P(Fraud) = 0.02, P(Legit) = 0.98, Sensitivity = 0.95 (True Positive Rate), Specificity = 0.95 (True Negative Rate). P(Fraud|Flagged) = P(Flagged|Fraud)×P(Fraud) / P(Flagged). P(Flagged) = (0.95×0.02) + (0.05×0.98) = 0.019 + 0.049 = 0.068. P(Fraud|Flagged) = (0.95×0.02)/0.068 = 0.019/0.068 ≈ 27.9%. Despite 95% model accuracy, only ~28% of flagged cases are truly fraudulent — this is the base rate fallacy.
cases.model_output: Applying Bayes' theorem: Prior P(Fraud)=0.02, P(Legitimate)=0.98. True positive rate=0.95, False positive rate=0.05. P(Flagged) = 0.95×0.02 + 0.05×0.98 = 0.019 + 0.049 = 0.068. Posterior P(Fraud|Flagged) = 0.019/0.068 ≈ 27.9%. Insight: even with 95% accuracy, precision is only 27.9% due to class imbalance. This illustrates the base rate fallacy — a critical consideration in fraud detection systems.
